summary: Riccomini examines the recent open source catalog announcements from Databricks (Unity) and Snowflake (Polaris), finding both fall far short of their marketing promises. Snowflake's Polaris Github repository is essentially empty despite extensive marketing materials. Databricks released an API-compatible rewrite of Unity rather than their actual product, missing critical features like audit capabilities and ML/AI governance. A third-party Rust rewrite (unity-rs) has also emerged, but it's barely started. Riccomini concludes that while these projects will likely mature, the vendors appear to be flailing without a clear strategy.
key_points: Snowflake's Polaris catalog was announced with significant marketing but the Github repository contains no actual code yet
Databricks open sourced an API-compatible rewrite of Unity rather than their production system, missing key features like audit capabilities and external storage support
A third-party Rust rewrite (unity-rs) has emerged but is essentially just a hello world file
Despite the challenges, extracting internal projects is genuinely difficult and rewrites are often the right approach
The vendors appear to lack a clear, articulated strategy for why they're open sourcing these catalogs
money_quotes: Yes, but have you looked at the code?
Out of all of these announcements, all this marketing, and all this noise, we've gotten one partial re-write, one empty Github project, and one hello world Rust file.
It appears the vendors have gotten ahead of themselves.
I'm not even sure they all understand why they're open sourcing these projects.
I'd love to see someone write down their data lakehouse strategy, or at least do a better job of communicating what the end state of all this is.
Right now it looks like flailing.
It's often hard to extricate internal projects. A rewrite is often the right move.
key_insight: The gap between vendor marketing and actual open source deliverables in the data lakehouse catalog space suggests these companies are rushing to stake claims without clear strategies or substantial code to back up their announcements.
